startScreenOptions.title: AN19.1-AN19.7 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for AN19.1-AN19.7.

interactions.label: Why does the foot remain motionless when the calf is squeezed in a patient with

params.question: Why does the foot remain motionless when the calf is squeezed in a patient with a ruptured calcaneal tendon?

answers.text: The tibial nerve is severed at the rupture site, preventing motor signals from reaching the foot

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The tibial nerve is not damaged in a tendon rupture. The Thompson test assesses the mechanical integrity of the calcaneal tendon, not nerve function.

answers.text: Severe pain inhibits voluntary contraction of the calf muscles

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Thompson test does not rely on voluntary contraction. The examiner passively squeezes the calf, and an intact tendon transmits that force to plantarflex the foot.

answers.text: The mechanical link between the calf muscles and the calcaneus is disrupted

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: When the calcaneal tendon ruptures, the squeeze force applied to the calf can no longer be transmitted to the calcaneus, so the foot stays still.

answers.text: The soleus muscle retracts proximally and can no longer generate adequate force

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Although the calf muscles may retract slightly, the fundamental problem is that the ruptured tendon no longer connects them to the heel bone.

overallFeedback.feedback: The Thompson test assesses mechanical continuity. Squeezing the calf transmits force through an intact calcaneal tendon to plantarflex the foot. When ruptured, that connection is lost and the foot does not move.
Exactly right. The Thompson test detects whether the calcaneal tendon can transmit the calf squeeze to the foot. A rupture breaks that mechanical link, so the foot does not plantarflex.

metadata.title: Why does the foot remain motionless when

interactions.label: A patient presents with gradual loss of the medial longitudinal arch in one foot

params.question: A patient presents with gradual loss of the medial longitudinal arch in one foot. Which structure's failure in the deep posterior compartment best explains this finding?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tibialis posterior is the primary invertor and the chief dynamic support of the medial arch. When its tendon fails, the arch collapses, causing adult-acquired flat foot.
Flexor hallucis longus powers great toe push-off. While it contributes to arch support, its failure does not cause adult-acquired flat foot.
The popliteus acts at the knee, internally rotating the tibia to unlock it from full extension. It plays no role in foot arch support.
Flexor digitorum longus flexes the lateral four toes and assists ground grip, but it is not the primary dynamic support of the medial arch.

overallFeedback.feedback: The tibialis posterior is the primary invertor and the most important dynamic support of the medial longitudinal arch. When its tendon fails, the arch collapses — a condition called adult-acquired flat foot.
Correct. Tibialis posterior is the primary invertor and key dynamic stabiliser of the medial arch. Its dysfunction is the leading cause of adult-acquired flat foot.

metadata.title: A patient presents with gradual loss of

interactions.label: Which mechanism explains why prolonged immobility increases the risk of deep vei

params.question: Which mechanism explains why prolonged immobility increases the risk of deep vein thrombosis in the calf?

answers.text: Inactivity causes vasoconstriction in the posterior tibial artery, reducing overall venous return

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: DVT arises from venous stasis, not arterial vasoconstriction. The calf pump acts on the venous side of the circulation.

answers.text: Loss of the calf muscle pump leads to blood stagnation in the soleal venous sinuses

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The soleus contains large venous sinuses that rely on calf contraction to propel blood upward. During immobility, blood pools in these sinuses, creating ideal conditions for clot formation.

answers.text: Knee extension during bed rest compresses the popliteal vein against the femoral condyles

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The popliteal vein is not compressed by knee extension. The DVT risk lies in venous stasis within the soleal sinuses due to absent calf pump action.

answers.text: Immobility triggers complete venous valve closure, blocking forward flow in perforating veins

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Venous valves prevent backflow; they do not close to block forward flow. Stasis occurs because the calf pump is inactive, not because valves obstruct flow.

overallFeedback.feedback: The calf muscles act as the 'peripheral heart.' During walking, their contraction squeezes blood out of the soleal venous sinuses and upward through valved veins. Immobility halts this pump, causing stasis and predisposing to DVT.
Exactly right. The calf muscles act as a 'peripheral heart' — their contractions squeeze the soleal venous sinuses and drive blood upward. Without this pump, blood stagnates and clots form.

metadata.title: Which mechanism explains why prolonged i

interactions.label: The medial plantar nerve is compared to the median nerve of the hand, while the

params.question: The medial plantar nerve is compared to the median nerve of the hand, while the lateral plantar nerve is compared to the ulnar nerve. What is the basis of this comparison?

answers.text: Both pairs of nerves originate from identical spinal cord segments

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The plantar nerves arise from the tibial nerve (L4–S3), while the median and ulnar nerves arise from the brachial plexus. The comparison is functional, not segmental.

answers.text: Both comparisons are based solely on sensory territory, not motor distribution

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While there are sensory parallels, the comparison is primarily based on the motor distribution pattern — the lateral plantar nerve controls the majority of intrinsic foot muscles.

answers.text: The medial plantar nerve supplies fewer muscles, similar to the median nerve in the forearm

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The comparison is about intrinsic muscle supply in the hand and foot, not the forearm. The medial plantar nerve supplies four muscles (LAFF mnemonic); the lateral plantar nerve supplies all the rest.

answers.text: The lateral plantar nerve supplies most intrinsic foot muscles, just as the ulnar nerve supplies most intrinsic hand muscles

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The lateral plantar nerve supplies all intrinsic foot muscles except the four covered by the medial plantar nerve (LAFF: first Lumbrical, Abductor hallucis, Flexor digitorum brevis, Flexor hallucis brevis). This mirrors the ulnar nerve's dominance over intrinsic hand muscles.

overallFeedback.feedback: The comparison is based on motor distribution. The lateral plantar nerve supplies all intrinsic foot muscles except the four supplied by the medial plantar nerve (remembered as LAFF). This mirrors the ulnar nerve, which supplies most intrinsic hand muscles.
Correct. The lateral plantar nerve is the motor workhorse of the sole, supplying all intrinsic muscles except the four in the LAFF group — exactly paralleling the ulnar nerve's dominance in the hand.

metadata.title: The medial plantar nerve is compared to

interactions.label: In plantar fasciitis, the calcaneal spur visible on X-ray is the primary cause o

params.question: In plantar fasciitis, the calcaneal spur visible on X-ray is the primary cause of the patient's heel pain.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The calcaneal spur is not the cause of pain. Although about half of cases show a spur on X-ray, the pain comes from repetitive microtrauma and degeneration at the plantar fascia's attachment to the medial calcaneal tubercle. The spur is an incidental finding.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. About half of plantar fasciitis patients show a calcaneal spur on X-ray, but the pain arises from chronic degenerative changes at the plantar fascia's calcaneal origin due to repetitive microtrauma, not from the spur itself.
